## A.4. Proof of Theorem 4

Without loss of generality, normalize so that U 1 ( ·| r ) = U 1 ( ·| r ′ ) for all r , r ′ . Suppose U k ( ·| r ) /negationslash= U k ( ·| r ′ ) for some r , r ′ and some k . Then, let ¯ /epsilon1 = d ( r , r ′ ) and pick a sequence ˆ rn →ˆ r such that: U k ( ·|ˆ rn ) /negationslash= U k ( ·| r ), ˆ rn ∈ B ¯ /epsilon1 ( r ) for all n , and d ( ˆ rn , r ) → inf { d ( r ′ , r ) : U k ( ·| r ) /negationslash= U k ( ·| r ′ ) } . Since ˆ rn ∈ cl ( B ¯ /epsilon1 ( r )), there is no loss in assuming this sequence converges. Similarly, let rn be a sequence in B ¯ /epsilon1 ( r ) such that rn →ˆ r and U k ( ·| r ) = U k ( ·| rn ).

By hypothesis and that each K k ( r ) is open, there exists /epsilon1&gt; 0, x k and x 1 such that B 2 /epsilon1 ( x k ) ⊂ K k ( ˆ r ), B 2 /epsilon1 ( x 1 ) ⊂ K 1 ( ˆ r ), and x k ∼ˆ r x 1 . By continuity of the region functions, B /epsilon1 ( x k ) ⊆ K i ( ˆ rn ) ∩ K i ( rn ) and B /epsilon1 ( x 1 ) ⊆ K 1 ( ˆ rn ) ∩ K 1 ( rn ) for n large enough. For z close enough to x k , there exists y ( z ) ∈ B /epsilon1 ( x 1 ) such that z ∼ˆ r y ( z ). But then by SC, z ∼ rn y ( z ) and z ∼ˆ rn y ( z ). Thus U k ( z | rn ) = U 1 ( y ( z ) | rn ) = U 1 ( y ( z ) |ˆ rn ) = U k ( z |ˆ rn ) for all z close enough to xk , implying that U k ( ·| rn ) = U k ( ·|ˆ rn ), a contradiction. Conclude U k ( ·| r ) = U k ( ·| r ′ ) for all r , r ′ . /square

## A.5. Examples from Table 1

Example 1 shows that BGS violates Cancellation and inspecting Figure 1 shows it violates Monotonicity. It remains to show that TK violates Reference Irrelevance and that MO violates Cancellation. This is established by the following two examples.

Example 2 (TK violates Reference Irrelevance). Consider a TK model with λ 1 = λ 2 = 2 . Then, for r = (10 , 10) , x = (12 , 12) , and y = (9 , 16) , y /followsorequal r x since (12 -10) + (12 -10) = 2(9 -10) + (16 -10) . For r ′ = (11 , 11) , x /follows r y since (12 -11) + (12 -11) &gt; 2(9 -11) + (16 -11) . But x ∈ R GL 1 ( r ) ⋂ R GL 1 ( r ′ ) andr ∈ R GL 2 ( r ) ⋂ R GL 2 ( r ′ ) , so the family violates Reference Irrelevance.

Example 3 (MO violates Cancellation). Let Q ( r ) ={ x ∈ X : x 1 / 2 + x 2 &gt; r 1 / 2 + r 2 } and c ( r ) = 1 . Then, let x = (2 , 1) , y = (1 , 2) , z = (4 , 4) , and r = (0 . 9 , 1 . 9) . Since ( x 1 , z 2) = (2 , 4) /followsorequal r (4 , 2) = ( z 1 , y 2) and ( z 1 , x 2) = (4 , 1) /followsorequal r (1 , 4) = ( y 1 , z 2) because all four points belong to Q ( r ) , cancellation requires that x /followsorequal r y. However, x / ∈ Q ( r ) , so y /follows r x, so cancellation does not hold.

## A.6. Other CTM

- A.6.1. Quasi-hyperbolic Model. Let the pair ( c , t ) represent consumption of c at time t . Formally, we define categories according to K QH = ( K short , K long ) where K short ( rc , rt ) ={ ( c , t ) | t &lt; rt } and K long ( r ) ={ ( c , t ) | t &gt; rt } . The utility function is

where 0 &lt;δ&lt; 1 and 0 &lt;β ≤ 1. The model is additively separable after taking logs, so it is a special case of CTM. It exhibits present bias when β&lt; 1: there exist values c &gt; c ′ &gt; 0 so that the DM prefers ( c ,τ ) /followsorequal r ( c ′ ,τ + 1) if and only if τ &lt; rt -1. 30 Figure A1 plots its indifference curves.

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- A.6.2. Inequality aversion model. The category function is K RIA = ( K E , K G ) where

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The set K G ( r ) contains all allocations where individual 1 is advantaged relative to the individual 2, and K E ( r ) all those where she is disadvantaged. 31

30. For instance u ( c ) = 1 and u ( c ′ ) = ( βδ ) -1 .
31. In general there are 2 n -1 categories, corresponding to envy or guilt for each binary comparison with every other individual. For instance, there are four categories with n = 3: EE , EG , GE , and GG .

Time

Time Preference

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Figure A1 CTM for dated rewards.

The DM feels guilty if her own relative gain is higher than the other's relative gain. Otherwise, the DM is envious of the other. Hence, a social allocation x is evaluated according to

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where α ≥ β ≥ 0 and β&lt; 1. Observe that when ri = rj for all i and j (the equitable outcome), the utility function reduces to that of Fehr and Schmidt (1999). Also, the model is an Affine CTM, and a Strong CTM for the restricted set of reference points with r 1 = r 2.

- A.6.3. Distributional Preferences:. Charness and Rabin (2002) argue that people care about both their own utility and social welfare as a whole. They maximize a weighted average of the two. Social welfare is a weighted average of society's total utility and the utility of worst-off individual. We propose a natural extension of their model with an exogenously given reference point. We call this model Reference-Dependent Distributional Preferences (RDDP) . Formally, categories are given by K CR = ( K 1 , K 2 ) where

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Each category corresponds to the individual with the worst relative payoff. If j = 1, then the DM is behind and wants to catch up. If j = 2, then the DM is ahead and is more willing to help the other to catch up. 32

In RDDP, the DM puts extra weight on the consumption of the individual who is furthest behind. Formally, she evaluates a social allocation x with reference r according to

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

∑ where δ,λ ∈ (0 , 1). Utility is increasing in the DM's own consumption, the minimum of all individuals' payoffs, and the total of all individuals' payoffs. Hence, the DM is willing to give up more of her own consumption to increase that of the worst-off individual than that of one of others. The parameter δ measures the degree of concern for helping the worst-off individual (Rawlsian) versus maximizing the total social payoffs (Utilitarian), and λ measures how the DM balances

32. With n individuals, there are n categories, each corresponding to the identity of the worst-treated individual in terms of relative consumption, ( xi -ri ). The utility function reduce to VCR ( x | r ) = (1 -λ )( x 1 -r 1) + λ [ δ min { x 1 -r 1 ,..., xn -rn }+ (1 -δ ) ∑ k ( xk -rk ) ] . While RDDP and RIA have category functions that coincide with n = 2 individuals, their category functions diverge for all other n . Figure A2 reveals that the behaviour necessarily differs even with n = 2.

Left: Relative Inequality Aversion and Right: Reference-Dependent Distributional Preferences.

social welfare with her own material payoff. Note that if ri = rj for all i and j , the utility function is cardinally equivalent to that of Charness and Rabin (2002). 33 The model is an Affine CTM.

## A.7. Other models and CTM

In this subsection, we present the functional forms of the other models we discussed, and show that they are not CTM.

- Gabaix (2014) assumes a rational DM would maximize u ( a , w ) but actually maximizes

where /Lambda1 ij incorporates the 'variance' in the marginal utility of dimensions i and j . When n is large, m ∗ i is often zero, so ( w 1 m ∗ 1 ,..., wnm ∗ n ) is a 'sparse' vector.

where vi is concave above 0 and convex below

- Bordalo et al. (2020) and the continuous form of the salient thinking model has

where w has the same properties as a salience function.

- Munro and Sugden (2003) use the functional from

33. The authors assume U ( x ) = (1 -λ ) x 1 + λ [ δ min { x 1 ,..., xn }+ (1 -δ ) ∑ k xk ] (see their Appendix 1). Pick any allocation x andreferencepoint r sothat rj = rk = r ∗ for every j and k . Let i ∗ ∈ argmin j xj . Subtracting the same constant from each element in a set does not change the minimizer, so VCR ( x | r ) = (1 -λ )( x 1 -r 1) + λδ ( xi ∗ -ri ∗ ) + λ (1 -δ ) ∑ k ( xk -rk ) = U ( x ) -λδ ri ∗ -(1 -λ ) r 1 -λ ∑ k rk . Since rj = r ∗ for all j , VCR ( x | r ) = U ( x ) -( λ n + (1 -λ (1 -δ ))) r ∗ , i.e. it is an affine transformation of U ( x ) and this transformation does not depend on x .

- Bhatia and Golman (2013) assume that the DM chooses the bundle x that maximizes

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

given that a reference point r , where each α i is increasing and positive.

The first fails to be CTM, as the indifference curves have the same slope everywhere for a fixed context. If they were CTM, then they would necessarily have only a single region. Single region CTM coincides with the neoclassical model. The final four explicitly take into account a reference point. In all four, it is easy to see that the reference point affects the marginal rate of substitution between attributes. This implies a violation of weak reference irrelevance for any given category function: any two points in the same category that are indifferent to each other necessarily remain so for a sufficiently small change in the reference point.

A.7.1. Non-increasing CTM. For simplicity, we have so far focused on increasing CTM. This is a desirable feature in consumer choice, but models of social preference often violate this property. For instance, inequality-averse individual 1 prefers to increase the allocation to individual 2 from x to y when she feels guilty but not when she is envious. However, she always prefers increasing the allocation to 2 in an allocation categorized as guilty, and to decrease in any categorized as envious. This contradicts Category Montonicity, suggesting the following weakening.

Axiom. (Consistent Preference within Category (CPC)). For each category k, there exists a set of attributes P k so that if x j ≥ yj for all j ∈ P k , yi ≥ xi for all i / ∈ P k , and x /negationslash= y, then y /followsorequal k ∗ x.

/negationslash

The set P k contains the attributes for which an increase positively affects the DM's evaluation. CPC requires that the set of positive attributes in a category does not depend on the reference point. For the two-person-RIA model, the set for the 'guilty' category is { 1 , 2 } since she strictly prefers increasing everyone's allocation, but the set for the 'envious' one is { 1 } -she prefers more for herself but dislikes others having even more. Note that CM is the special case of CPC where P k includes every dimension for every category.

ACTMischaracterized by all the properties of an increasing CTM, except where CM is replaced by CPC. The proof is a straightforward generalization of earlier one, so it is omitted.

## A.8. Proof of Proposition 1

Suppose that { /followsorequal r } r ∈ X has a CTM and fix a category k with LIS k ( x ) /negationslash= LIS l ( x ) for every x ∈ X and category l /negationslash= k . Consider a category k and reference r . Define

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

To show the reverse inclusion, suppose that x ∈ K l ( r ) for category l /negationslash= k . Since LIS k ( x ) /negationslash= LIS l ( x ), for any neighbourhood O /owner x there exists y ∈ O so that either U k ( y ) /negationslash= U k ( x ) and U l ( x ) = U l ( y ) or U k ( y ) = U k ( x ) and U l ( x ) /negationslash= U l ( y ). In particular, this applies to O ′ = O ∩ K l ( r ), so either there exists y ∈ O ′ so that either y ∼ r x and U k ( y ) /negationslash= U k ( x ) (in the first case) or y /negationslash∼ r x and U k ( y ) = U k ( x ) (in the second). Hence, x / ∈ K . Since x is arbitrary, we have K l ( r ) ∩ K =∅ . Since K l is open, we have int ( K ) ∩ cl ( K l ( r )) =∅ . Since l was arbitrary, cl ( ⋃ l /negationslash= k K l ( r )) ∩ int ( K ) = ⋃ l /negationslash= k cl ( K l ( r )) ∩ int ( K ) =∅ since there are finitely many categories. Since the categories are dense, int ( K ) ⊂ cl ( K k ( r )), and it follows that int ( K ) ⊂ int ( cl ( K k ( r ))) = K k ( r ) since K k ( r ) is a regular open set. Conclude int ( K ) = K k ( r ), and that we can identify K k ( r ) for any k and r .

We show int ( K ) = K k ( r ). Let x ∈ K k ( r ). Then, there exists a neighbourhood O /owner x with O ⊂ K k ( r ) since K k ( r ) open. By the representation, for any y ∈ O , x ∼ r y if and only if U k ( y ) = U k ( x ), so picking any /epsilon1&gt; 0 so that B /epsilon1 ( x ) ⊂ O shows that x ∈ K . Since K k ( r ) is open and K k ( r ) ⊂ K , K k ( r ) ⊂ int ( K ).

Proposition 6 Let { /followsorequal r } r ∈ X be a CTM where each category is connected. For any reference r such that U k ( x | r ) /negationslash= U l ( x | r ) for every x ∈ X and categories l , k with l /negationslash= k, the category function is uniquely identified for r.

Proof. For any x ∗ , let O be the ⊆ -largest connected, open set connected so that for every x ∈ O there is an /epsilon1&gt; 0 so that { y ∈ B /epsilon1 ( x ) : y /followsorequal r z } and { y ∈ B /epsilon1 ( x ) : z /followsorequal r y } are closed for each z ∈ B /epsilon1 ( x ). A maximal set with this property exists by Zorn's Lemma, and is unique since any two such maximal sets contain x ∗ , so their union is also a maximal set. If x ∗ ∈ K k ( r ), we claim that O = K k ( r ).

Now, K k ( r ) and K -k ( r ) = ⋃ l /negationslash= k K l ( r ) are disjoint, open sets whose union contains O . Hence, either K k ( r ) ∩ O =∅ or K -k ( r ) ∩ O =∅ . The former is impossible, so K k ( r ) ⊇ O . But clearly K k ( r ) is a connected, open set satisfying the condition of O , so K k ( r ) ⊆ O . Conclude K k ( r ) = O . /square

First, note O ⊂ ⋃ n l = 1 K l ( r ), where n is the number of categories. For x ∈ X so that x / ∈ ⋃ n l = 1 K l ( r ), there are categories i , j so that x ∈ bdK i ( r ) and x ∈ bdK j ( r ). WLOG, U i ( x | r ) &gt; U j ( x | r ). For any /epsilon1&gt; 0, there exists x ′ ∈ K i ( r ) ∩ B /epsilon1 ( x ) with U i ( x | r ) &gt; U i ( x ′ | r ) &gt; U j ( x | r ), x ′′ ∈ K j ( r ) ∩ B /epsilon1 ( x ) with U i ( x | r ) &gt; U j ( x ′′ | r ) &gt; U j ( x | r ), and sequences x ′ n ∈ K i ( r ) ∩ B /epsilon1 ( x ) and x ′′ n ∈ K j ( r ) ∩ B /epsilon1 ( x ) so that x ′ n → x and x ′′ n → x . Since x ′ /follows r x ′′ , either x ′ /follows r x or x /follows r x ′′ . In the former case, x ′ n /followsorequal r x ′ for all n large enough but x ′ /follows r x ; in the latter, x ′′ /followsorequal r x ′′ n for all n large enough but x /follows r x ′′ . In either case we obtain a contradiction.

Consider the following category utility functions. U 1 ( x 1 , x 2 | r ) = x 1 + x 2 and

For any category function, the boundary between the categories can be identified on the set { x : x 1 + x 2 &gt; 1 } but not on the other points. Intuitively, the DM evaluates objects in this set the same regardless of their categorization and so we cannot hope to identify their category. When the category does not affect the DM's choice, the revealed preference approach cannot distinguish the two and non-choice data must be used.

Thereis no loss in normalizing so that β 1 = 0. Pick x ∈ X with x 1 &gt; x 2, and by S4 observe that x ∈ K 1 ( r ) for r = ( x 1 , x 2 / 2) and x ∈ K 2 ( r ′ ) for r ′ = ( x 1 / 2 , x 2). Since K 1 ( r ) and K 2 ( r ′ ) are open, there exists /epsilon1&gt; 0 so that B /epsilon1 ( x ) ⊂ K 1 ( r ) ⋂ K 2 ( r ). Since U 1 is continuous and increasing, there is y ∈ B /epsilon1 ( x ) with y 1 &lt; x 1 so that U 1 ( y ) = U 1 ( x ), i.e. , y ∼ r x ; this y necessarily has y 2 &gt; x 2 by CM. Then, SDO implies y /follows r ′ x , i.e. , U 2 ( y ) &gt; U 2 ( x ), which requires w 1 2 / w 2 2 &lt; w 1 1 &gt; w 2 1 . We can incorporate β 2 into u 2 by replacing it with u 2 + β 2 / ( w 2 2 -w 1 2 ) or into u 1 by replacing β 2 into u 1 by replacing it with u 1 + β 2 / ( w 2 1 -w 1 1 ). At least one does not involve dividing by zero, as otherwise w 2 i = w 1 i for i = 1 , 2. /square

## A.11. TK

This subsection states and proves a characterization theorem for TK.

Proposition 7 Afamily of preferences { /followsorequal r } r ∈ X has a TK representation if and only if it is an Affine CTM with a gain-loss regional function that satisfies Reference Interlocking, Monotonicity, Cancellation, and continuity of each /followsorequal r .

Tversky and Kahneman (1991, p. 1053) provide an alternative axiomatic characterization of the model, and our result makes heavy use of their theorem.

Proof. Necessity follows from the discussion above and TK's theorem. To show sufficiency, we rely on TK's theorem, which states that any monotone, continuous family of preference relations that satisfies cancellation, sign-dependence, and reference interlocking has a TK representation. Given our assumptions, we need to show that { /followsorequal r } satisfies signdependence and reference interlocking.

TK say that { /followsorequal r } satisfies sign-dependence if 'for any x , y , r , s ∈ X , x /followsorequal r y ⇐⇒ x /followsorequal s y whenever x and y belong to the same quadrant with respect to r and with respect to s , and r and s belong to the same quadrant with respect to x and with respect to y .' This happens if and only if x ∈ Kk ( r ) ⋂ K k ( s ) and y ∈ K k ( r ) ⋂ K k ( s ) for some k ∈{ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 } . Then, sign-dependence is exactly an implication of Affine CTM, since U k ( ·| r ) = α U k ( ·| s ) + β for α&gt; 0.

TKsay that { /followsorequal r } satisfies reference interlocking if 'for any w , w ′ , x , x ′ , y , y ′ , z , z ′ that belong to the same quadrant with respect to r as well as with respect to s , w 1 = w ′ 1 , x 1 = x ′ 1 , y 1 = y ′ 1 , z 1 = z ′ 1 , and x 2 = z 2 , w 2 = y 2 , x ′ 2 = z ′ 2 , w ′ 2 = y ′ 2 , if w ∼ r x , y ∼ r z , and w ′ ∼ s x ′ then y ′ ∼ s z ′ .' The assumptions on quadrants imply that w , w ′ , x , x ′ , y , y ′ , z , z ′ ∈ K k ( r ) ⋂ K l ( s ) for some k , l ∈{ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 } . Since y ′ , z ′ ∈ K l ( s ), the conclusion follows immediately from RI. /square

Standard results then show existence of a continuous function σ so that ( a , b ) S ( c , d ) if and only if σ ( a , b ) ≥ σ ( c , d ). σ is symmetric by S2 and increasing in contrast by S1 and S4. Hence, x ∈ K 1 ( y ) if and only if σ ( x 1 , y 1) &gt;σ ( x 2 , y 2), and by S2, x ∈ K 2 ( y ) if and only if y ′ ∈ K 1 ( x ′ ) where x ′ , y ′ are the reflections of x , y . Pick any x , y &gt; 0. We claim that ( x , y ) / ∈ K 1 ( x , y ) ∪ K 2 ( x , y ), and hence σ ( x , x ) = σ ( y , y ). Observe that ( x + /epsilon1 , y ) ∈ K 1 ( x , y ) and( x , y + /epsilon1 ) ∈ K 2 ( x , y ) by S4 for /epsilon1 /negationslash= 0. By S0, K 1 ( x , y ) , K 2 ( x , y ) are open, so ( x , y ) / ∈ K 1 ( x , y ) and ( x , y ) / ∈ K 2 ( x , y ). Conclude σ is grounded.

Pick any a , b . By S3, σ ( a , b ) = σ ( b , a ) so ( a , b ) / ∈ K 1 ( b , a ) for any a , b . By S5, ( a + /epsilon1 , b ) / ∈ K 1 ( b + /epsilon1 , a ). Then, ( b , a ) S ( a + /epsilon1 , b + /epsilon1 ) so σ ( a , b ) = σ ( b , a ) ≥ σ ( a + /epsilon1 , b + /epsilon1 ). Since a , b were arbitrary, diminishing sensitivity holds.

For necessity, verifying that S0-S5 hold are trivial, except that each K i ( r ) is regular open. To see this, pick r and x ∈ int ( cl ( K 1 ( r ))) (symmetric arguments hold for K 2 ). Suppose x /greatermuch r (the other cases follow by changing the signs). Then, there are /epsilon1 1 ,/epsilon1 2 such that ( x 1 -r 1) / 2 &gt;/epsilon1 1 &gt; 0 ,/epsilon1 2 &gt; 0 so that ¯ x = ( x 1 -/epsilon1 1 , x 2 + /epsilon1 2) ∈ cl ( K 1 ( r )). Since there exists x ′ ∈ K 1 ( r ) that is arbitrarily close to ¯ x , we can find x ′ ∈ K 1 ( r ) so that | x ′ 1 -x 1 | &lt;/epsilon1 1 / 2 and | x ′ 2 -x 2 | &lt;/epsilon1 2 / 2. Moreover, σ ( x ′ 1 , r 1) &gt;σ ( x ′ 2 , r 2) since x ′ ∈ K 1 ( r ). These inequalities imply σ ( x 1 , r 1) &gt;σ ( x 2 , r 2), hence x ∈ K 1 ( r ). Now we show the following are equivalent:

- (i) The functions K 1 and K 2 satisfy S0, S1, and S6,
- (ii) There exists a salience function σ s.t. x ∈ K k ( r ) ⇐⇒ σ ( xk , rk ) &gt;σ ( x -k , r -k )

That (ii) implies (i) follows from the first part, and that S6 is implied by symmetry and homogeneity of degree zero. Now, we show (i) implies (ii). Set σ ( a , b ) = max { a / b , b / a } . so there exists λ ∈ (0 , 1) such that ( λ x 1 + (1 -λ ) r 1 , x 2) = ( r 1 x 2 / r 2 , x 2) = x ′ . By S1 and x ∈ K 2 ( r ), x ′ ∈ K 2 ( r ). and by S6, ( A ⋃ B ) c ⋂ K k ( r ) =∅ for k = 1 , 2. Thus, A = K 1 ( r ) and B = K 2 ( r ), completing the proof. Finally, fix any HOD salience function s . Thus, if one salience function generates the regions, every other salience function does as well. /square

## B. PROOFS AND EXTRAS FROM SECTION 5

## B.1. Axioms for c

This subsection formally states the adaptations of the axioms for reference dependent preferences { /followsorequal r } r ∈ X in terms of the choice correspondence c . Interpretation is identical to that of those axioms.

Axiom. (Category Cancellation*). For all x 1 , y 1 , z 1 , x 2 , y 2 , z 2 ∈ R ++ and category k: if ( x 1 , z 2) ∈ c ( S 1 ) , ( z 1 , y 2) ∈ S 1 , ( z 1 , x 2) ∈ c ( S 2 ) , ( y 1 , z 2) ∈ S 2 , ( x 1 , x 2) , ( y 1 , y 2) ∈ S 3 , and S i ⊂ K k ( A ( S i )) for i ∈{ 1 , 2 , 3 } , then ( x 1 , x 2) ∈ c ( S 3 ) whenever ( y 1 , y 2) ∈ c ( S 3 ) .

Axiom (Category Monotonicity*). For any x , y ∈ X: if x ≥ y and x /negationslash= y, then ( y , k ) /followsorequal R ( x , k ) for any category k.

/negationslash

Axiom (Category Continuity*). and any /epsilon1&gt; 0 so that E ⋂ S \ c ( S ) =∅ where E ≡ ⋃ x ∈ c ( S ) B /epsilon1 ( x ) there exists δ &gt; 0 so that if S ′ ∈ X , d ( A ( S ′ ) , A ( S )) &lt;δ , and for any y ′ ∈ S ′ , there is y ∈ S so that y ′ ∈ B δ ( y ) , then c ( S ′ ) ⊂ E whenever S ′ ⋂ E /negationslash=∅ .

Define /followsorequal R , k by x /followsorequal R , k y if and only if ( x , k ) /followsorequal R ( y , k ). Using this relation, we can define ⊕ k for each category as we did with preference relations.

Axiom (Affine Across Categories*). For any S 1 , S 2 , S 3 ∈ X , x i ∈ K j ( A ( S i )) , y i ∈ K k ( A ( S i )) for i = 1 , 2 , 3 , and any α ∈ (0 , 1) so that ( x 3 , j ) /followsorequal R ( α x 1 ⊕ j (1 -α ) x 2 , j ) and ( α y 1 ⊕ k (1 -α ) y 2 , k ) /followsorequal R ( y 3 , k ) : if x 1 ∈ c ( S 1 ) and x 2 ∈ c ( S 2 ) , then y 3 / ∈ c ( S 3 ) .

Axiom (Salient Dimension Overvalued*). For x , y ∈ S ⋂ S ′ with xk &gt; yk and y -k &gt; x -k , if x , y ∈ K k ( A ( S )) , x , y ∈ R -k ( A ( S ′ )) , and y ∈ c ( S ) , then x / ∈ c ( S ′ ) .

Axiom (Reference Interlocking*). For any a , b , a ′ , b ′ , x ′ , y ′ , x , y ∈ X with x -i = a -i , y -i = b -i , x ′ -i = a ′ -i , y ′ -i = b ′ -i , xi = x ′ i , yi = y ′ i , ai = a ′ i , bi = b ′ i : if x ∼ R ∗ k y, a /followsorequal R ∗ k b, and x ′ ∼ R ∗ j y ′ , then it does not hold that b ′ /follows R ∗ j a ′ .

## B.2. Proof of Theorem 6

Lemma 12 Assume that Revealed Structure holds, and that A is a generalized average. If Category-SARP, Category Monotonicity*, Category Cancellation*, and Category Continuity* hold, then for any category k there exists a Category utility U k so that for any x , y ∈ E R , k ,

CM* gives that either x ′ ∈ c ( S ∗ ) or y ′ ∈ c ( S ∗ ), so x ′ /followsorequal R , i y ′ or y ′ /followsorequal R , i x ′ .

By definition of E ∗ , for any z ∈ E ∗ , there exists S ∈ X with A ( S ) = r so that c ( S ) = z . Since K i ( r ) is open, there exists /epsilon1 1 &gt; 0 so that B 2 /epsilon1 1 ( z ) ⊂ K i ( r ). By Lemma 11, there exists /epsilon1 2 &gt; 0 so that r ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 2 ( r ) implies B /epsilon1 1 ( z ) ⊂ K i ( r ′ ). Pick ζ ∈ (0 , 1 2 ) so that B ζ ( z ) ∩ S = z . By Category Continuity*, there exists /epsilon1 3 &gt; 0 so that for any S ′ ∈ X with d ( A ( S ′ ) , A ( S )) &lt;/epsilon1 3, for any y ′ ∈ S ′ , there is y ∈ S so that y ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 3 ( y ), and S ′ ⋂ B ζ ( x ) /negationslash=∅ , then c ( S ′ ) ⊂ B ζ ( x ). By Generalized Average, there exists /epsilon1 4 &gt; 0 so that z ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 4 ( z ) implies d ( A ( S \{ z }∪{ z ′ } ) , A ( S )) &lt; min { /epsilon1 2 ,/epsilon1 3 } / 2. Let /epsilon1 ∗ = min { /epsilon1 1 ,/epsilon1 2 ,/epsilon1 3 ,/epsilon1 4 ,ζ } .

Continuity follows along the same lines as Lemma 2. CM* gives that it is also monotone, and Category Cancellation* that it is locally additive. Apply Theorem 2.2 of Chateauneuf and Wakker (1993) to get a globally additive representation U k . /square

By Lemma 12, there exists a category utility U k for each category. Since E R , k is dense in D k , we can extend U k to D k uniquely. By Generalized Average and Category Continuity*, for any S ∈ X with z ∈[ D k \ E R k ]∩ S , there is a z ′ ∈ E R , k arbitrarily close to z so that c ( S ) = c ( [ S \{ z }]∪{ z ′ } ), so it is sufficient to establish a representation when all alternatives categorized as k in S belong E R , k for each k and S .

Fix two regions k and j . By CAR, for any x ∈ E R , k there exists x ′ ∈ E R , k , y ∈ E R , j , and S ∈ X so that x ′ , y ∈ c ( S ) and x ∼ R , k x ′ . This implies there exists a strictly increasing function H so that V ( x | r ) = U k ( x ) when x ∈ K k ( r ) and V ( x | r ) = H ( U j ( x )) , when x ∈ K j ( r ) represents choice (when S ⊂ K k ⋃ K j ). This is well-defined and represents choice by Category SARP. By AAC*, H is an affine function. The argument are readily seen to extend inductively to all regions, which complete the proof. /square

## B.3. Proof of Lemma 1

Pick any x ∈ X and set S ={ x , x ′ } where x ′ = ( 1 2 x 1 , x 2). Then, A ( S )2 = x 2 by strong generalized average, so both x and x ′ are 1-salient by S4. By CM*, x ∈ c ( S ), and so x ∈ E R , 1 . x was arbitrary, so X = E R , 1 . Similar for K 2 . /square

## B.4. Proof of Proposition 4

By Lemma 1, the structure assumption is satisfied. By Theorem 5, the category function is generated by a salience function. By Theorem 6, c conforms to Strong CTM. Mimicking the arguments of Theorem 2, Reference Interlocking implies U k ( x ) = w k 1 u 1( x 1) + w k 2 u 2( x 2) + β k . The rest follows from the arguments that establish Proposition 3. /square

## B.5. Proof of Proposition 5

Suppose that c has two BGS representations, ( U 1 , U 2 ,σ ) and ( U ′ 1 , U ′ 2 ,σ ′ ). We first show that σ and σ ′ categorize all alternatives y /greatermuch r the same for every r . Then, we use symmetry to show this implies they agree everywhere. Finally, we show that we can also directly reveal the category of y .

For contradiction, assume that σ and σ ′ disagree on the category of y for reference r when y /greatermuch r : σ ′ ( yk , rk ) ≤ σ ′ ( y -k , r -k ) and σ ( yk , rk ) &gt;σ ( y -k , r -k ). By continuity and increasing differences, we can take both inequalities to be strict by lowering yk . Interchanging the role of the two representations if necessary, there is no loss in assuming that U k ( r ) ≥ U -k ( r ). By continuity, there exists /epsilon1&gt; 0 so that if d ( r ′ , r ) &lt;/epsilon1 and d ( y , y ′ ) &lt;/epsilon1 , then

Pick S so its convex hull is contained in B /epsilon1 ′ ( r ) and U k ( y ) &gt; U k ( z ) , U -k ( z ) for all z ∈ B /epsilon1 ′ + /epsilon1 ′ 2 ( r ) for some /epsilon1 ′ &lt;/epsilon1/ 2; /epsilon1 ′ exists by continuity of U k and U -k . Since A is a strong generalized average, d ( A ( S ) , r ) &lt;/epsilon1/ 2. For any y ′ , generalized

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

average implies there exists S ′ so that d ( A ( S ′ ) , A ( S )) &lt;/epsilon1 ′ , d ( S ′ \{ y ′ , y } , S ) &lt;/epsilon1 ′ 2 , and y , y ′ ∈ S ′ . Label it S ( y ′ ) and note d ( A ( S ( y ′ )) , r ) &lt;/epsilon1 .

We show that σ and σ ′ agree on the category of all alternatives whenever they agree whenever y /greatermuch r . Pick any x , y , a , b &gt; 0. We show that σ ( x , a ) &gt;σ ( y , a ) if and only if x ′ ∈ K 1 ( r ) for an appropriately chosen alternatives x ′ , r so that x ′ /greatermuch r . This is impossible if x = a and always true if y = b and x /negationslash= a . For any other values, it follows from symmetry of σ that σ ( x , a ) &gt;σ ( y , b ) if and only if either ( x , y ) ∈ K 1 ( a , b ), x &gt; a and y &gt; b ; ( x , b ) ∈ K 1 ( a , y ), x &gt; a , and b &gt; y ; ( a , y ) ∈ K 1 ( x , b ), x &lt; a , and y &gt; b ; or ( a , b ) ∈ K 1 ( x , y ), x &lt; a , and b &gt; y .

Pick y ′ with d ( y , y ′ ) &lt;/epsilon1 so that U k ( y ′ ) = U k ( y ) and y /negationslash= y ′ . As above, U -k ( y ) /negationslash= U -k ( y ′ ). By Lemma 1 and Theorem 2.2 of Chateauneuf and Wakker (1993), U ′ j and U j agree up to an affine transformation for j = 1 , 2, so U ′ k ( y ′ ) = U ′ k ( y ) and U ′-k ( y ) /negationslash= U ′-k ( y ′ ) also. Since ( U ′ 1 , U ′ 2 ,σ ′ ) represents c , y , y ′ ∈ K k ( A ( S ( y ′ ))) and c ( S ( y ′ )) ={ y , y ′ } . However, ( U ′ 1 , U ′ 2 ,σ ′ ) also represents c , so y , y ′ ∈ K -k ( A ( S ( y ′ ))). Hence, it is impossible that c ( S ( y ′ )) ={ y ′ , y } ; one has strictly higher utility than the other. This is a contradiction of both representing c , so conclude the categories coincide when y /greatermuch r .

Wefinally turn to directly revealing the salience of each alternative. As above, it suffices to consider y /greatermuch r and identify the categories of each alternative in U ( r ) ={ x : x /greatermuch r } . Again, pick k so that U k ( r ) ≥ U -k ( r ) and define S ( y ′ ) as above. If y ∈ K k ( r ), then there exists /epsilon1 ′ &gt; 0 so that y , y ′ ∈ K k ( A ( S ( y ′ )) whenever y ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 ′ ( y ). It follows that c ( S ( y ′ )) ={ y , y ′ } when U k ( y ) = U k ( y ′ ). If y ∈ K -k ( r ), then there exists /epsilon1 ′′ &gt; 0 so that y , y ′ ∈ K -k ( A ( S ( y ′ ))) for all y ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 ′′ ( y ). For any such y ′ with U k ( y ) = U k ( y ′ ) and y ′ /negationslash= y , c ( S ( y ′ )) /negationslash={ y , y ′ } : U -k ( y ) /negationslash= U -k ( y ′ ), so either y ′ is not chosen, y is not chosen, or both are not chosen, in which case one of the alternatives close to r is chosen. Since K k ( r ) ∪ K -k ( r ) is dense in U ( r ), K k ( r ) ∩ U ( r ) is the interior of the set of y /greatermuch r for which there exists an /epsilon1 ′ so that c ( S ( y ′ )) ={ y , y ′ } when U k ( y ) = U k ( y ′ ) and y ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 ′ ( y ), and K -k ( r ) ∩ U ( r ) is the interior of the set of y /greatermuch r for which there exists an /epsilon1 ′ so that c ( S ( y ′ )) /negationslash={ y , y ′ } when U k ( y ) = U k ( y ′ ) and y ′ ∈ B /epsilon1 ′ ( y ). /square
